summary: Gruber dismantles factual claims made in John Arlidge's profile of Jony Ive, specifically the assertion that Apple's stock price has slumped and lost its title as the world's most valuable company since Steve Jobs died. He shows that Apple's stock was actually up 39 percent since Jobs's death and that Apple was, at the time of publication, the world's most valuable company. Gruber argues these are trivially fact-checkable claims that both The Sunday Times and Time Magazine failed to verify. He frames this as an example of media 'truthiness' — publishing false claims because they fit a convenient narrative of Apple's decline.
key_points: Apple's stock was up 39 percent from Jobs's death to the article's publication, directly contradicting the claim of a slump
Apple was the world's most valuable company at the time of publication, the opposite of what Arlidge wrote
These facts were easily verifiable in about five minutes, yet two major publications ran the false claims unchecked
The errors persist because they fit the popular 'Apple is in decline without Jobs' narrative that media outlets find compelling
Even the stock's dip from its 2012 peak occurred well after Jobs's death, making the causal link dishonest
money_quotes: Apple's stock was, therefore, as of publication of this interview, up 39 percent since Steve Jobs died. I repeat: up 39 percent.
Apple, right now, today, is the world's most valuable firm, and has been for the last eight months or so.
It's fair to say Apple's share price has slumped, but you can't say it has slumped 'since Jobs died' — and that is the clear implication in Arlidge's piece.
Share prices and market cap values are easily fact-checked. It took me about five minutes to look up all the numbers in this piece.
Yet both The Sunday Times and Time Magazine published the above statement, which is both false and deeply misleading.
Who needs the truth when you've got truthiness on your side?
key_insight: Major publications repeatedly publish easily disproven claims about Apple's financial decline because the narrative of post-Jobs decay is too compelling to fact-check.
